I just looked at the CUDA code.  These kernels would be a lot faster if, rather than launching many of them you processed the entire tensor with one grid stride loop.  You also don't need the grid_stride_range_y loop.  You will get better hardware occupancy with one grid stride loop over everything.  The only time you need 2D grid stride loops is when you need to do something complex that has hardware limits on how the warps are structured.  For instance, I think the only time I've needed 2D grid stride loops is when doing complex warp reductions, which you aren't using here (and don't need).

We should **not** have caught `RuntimeError`. `RuntimeError` is thrown when we close a session, then call `run()` on it. We should never close a session then call `run()` on it as then the session is then in invalid state. Catching `RuntimeError` here was hiding part of the cause of the crashing bug.

Catching `tf.errors.CancelledError` seems reasonable, but I am not sure if this is the only thing we need to catch.

The return values are of course in the same order as they are "unpacked" in the unit test. I order them in the unit test (which I write first generally) in the order they are used. Then I copy that down to the return statement, and create them in the order they appear in the return statement, satisfying prerequisites as necessary. You seem to have picked that up, so just in case you were wondering why things showed up that way :)  I generally find it useful to be as consistent and mechanical as possible; it speeds the development process (like conforming to a kata) and of course makes it easier to follow months later when you look at it again :)

Ok, basically it looks like you haven't implemented the oxml layer yet. You shouldn't need any lxml code in this (API)  layer. So sectPr is a CT_SectPr type and is defined in docx.oxml.section. It has `w:headerReference` defined as a ZeroOrMore child, but the `w:headerReference` tag name is not registered in `docx/oxml/__init__.py`, so all headerReference elements get loaded as a regular lxml class with no custom behaviors.

In order to register it, you need a custom element class, CT_whatever, probably in docx/oxml/header.py. The class name is spelled the same as the XML complex type name. Then you can define attributes on it like id and rId.

The attribute definition would be:

```python
rId = OptionalAttribute('r:id', ST_RelationshipId)
```

Then getting the rId value is just `rId = headerReference.rId`. Note the convention is to spell the element variable name exactly as the tag name (less the namespace prefix), so headerReference rather than headerRef.

> I think the implication is that we might create something that is not transferable to other database backends outside of sqlite -- is that true?

There's no flavor-specific SQL in there (i.e. not tied to sqlite) -- just standard joins and WHERE conditions. We do want to use Kolibri with other DB backends (like Postgres), but I don't think this code in particular is more vulnerable to DB differences than standard Django code (e.g. we've seen issues in KA Lite of different DB level integrity enforcement (such as uniqueness) in sqlite vs mysql. So, I think we'll want to (occasionally) run the full test suite using these alternative database engines, once we start using them, but don't think it makes sense to try to integrate them into the test suite itself (would probably make for much slower tests).

I'll admit that the code here is a little confusing,  but it is in service of a readable clean API for developers:

```python
fibonacci.delay(20,  girder_client_token="....")
```
vs.

```python
fibonacci.delay(20,  girder_headers={ "girder_client_token": "...." })
```

What's more this makes the distinction between kwargs that are used to control behavior in the ```before_task_publish``` signal vs options that are passed through to the message headers.  This means you would need a dict for ```girder_headers``` and a dict for ```girder_options```. This places unnecessary burden on the API user to know the difference between these things *or* means you've got to parse it out for them inside Task class. 

Each option that is possible has to be handled inside the Task code anyways to provide sensible defaults (in the case of kwargs being passed headers) or to provide functionality (in the case of kwargs effecting signal functionality).

There are several ways to concatenate strings in python: `+`, `%`, `format`, `join`... Of these, `format` is the only one that does not automatically upgrade the output type to text type if one of the inputs happens to be text type. For that reason, we avoid using it in Girder and prefer `%` for formatting even though it lacks power -- if one forgets to prepend the `u` type specifier to the template string, it can cause exceptions to occur that won't be caught in testing unless you test with strings outside the range of ASCII.

This felt like a good time to break that rule because of the variable template. However, consistency is desirable. I'm fine with having them both use one way or the other, but if `format` is the way we want to go everywhere, we need to be disciplined about enforcing the `u` before the template string. I would go as far as wanting a lint rule for it if that's possible.

Interval computations are conservative, so the intervals should be no larger than any specific residual. In this case we have the same computed quantity (V + I*Rs) showing up twice in the current sum residual computation, and this in known to cause the intervals to be "pessimistic" because the two quantities are treated independently in the overall computation, which they are not. - Remove the `mDisableRadioOnIdle`

Would be reasonable to assume that `aInputLength` will always be larger than `*aOutputLength`? If that is always the case, then you could save a significant amount of memory (Getting rid of `NcpFrameBufferTransformerReader::mInputBuffer`) by requiring implementations of this method to support in-place transformation (i.e. where `aInput == aOutput`). This seems like it would be a reasonable requirement to place on a decryption implementation, since presumably the encrypted frame would have several bytes of header and if you are using something like AES-CCM you can decrypt on a byte-by-byte basis.

On that note, we could make `TransformOutbound` work similarly if we added a way for the caller to determine how large the header and footer of an encrypted frame would be. That would allow the caller to make sure that the plaintext frame is in the exact right spot for in-place encryption, allowing us to get rid of `NcpFrameBufferTransformerReader::mOutputBuffer` too.

It shouldn't treat them as fatal, but nor should it make it easy to treat a query failure in precisely the same way as a definitely unrevoked cert. It's python, exceptions aren't fatal. https://jeffknupp.com/blog/2013/02/06/write-cleaner-python-use-exceptions/

Written this way, in idiomatic checks, this function will check if the cert is revoked or unknown, not revoked or unrevoked. Which works for how it's called now, but would require refactoring were we ever to want to use it again. That is, people will call it as:

```
if ocsp_revoked:
  do_something()
```
which works fine, but
```
if not ocsp_revoked():
  do_something_else()
```
is not analogous and might lead to future buggy code.

There are a couple problems with this:
- Currently, the way snow_ice_temperature_poly is used, you only ever have a constant value for the snow+ice temperature. It is initialized with a CubicSpline that has a constant profile, and always returns a CubicSpline with a constant profile in the diagnostics of IceSheet. This tells me that it should just use a constant value instead.
- The second problem is that this breaks the Sympl API around units. The units on this quantity are currently set to 'dimensionless', even though they should be Kelvin. If you did set the units, then unit conversions would not work, since CubicSpline objects can't be unit converted.
- It's also not immediately clear what the CubicSpline takes as input (it takes grid indices).
- There is also a bug here - when you have a growing layer let's say of size N last timestep, now size N+1, [this line](https://github.com/CliMT/climt/blob/develop/climt/_components/surface_ice.py#L215) will try to get the temperature at cell N+1 from a CubicSpline that only has values set from 0 to N. It will do this by cubic extrapolation, which is probably not what you want, since it uses the same cubic function at point N in the spline as at point N-1. None of this is clear from looking at the default value or at the _properties dictionary.

I think the way to do it that's compatible with Sympl would be either:
- If you really do only need constant temperatures, just use a single value.
- If you need non-constant temperatures, you can have DataArrays whose dimension lengths change from timestep to timestep. I think this would require some modifications to Sympl (to allow a dimension to change size), but that would be perfectly reasonable. This is something that we should make Sympl compatible with, anyways.
